did_this_paper_use_ai_to_obtain_results_.details: The paper used a transfer learning-based convolutional neural network (CNN) with ResNet18 architecture to classify optical coherence tomography (OCT) images of basal cell carcinoma (BCC) and squamous cell carcinoma (SCC) versus normal skin. The AI model was used to determine the probability of lesions being cancerous and to identify clear lesion edges noninvasively.

is_this_paper_a_review_paper_or_a_resear.details: The paper presents original experimental work involving the development and validation of a transfer learning-based convolutional neural network (CNN) using ResNet18 architecture to classify optical coherence tomography (OCT) images of basal cell and squamous cell carcinomas. It includes specific methods (patient image collection, 3D reconstruction, model training with 5-fold cross-validation), results (sensitivity/specificity between 92-100%, specific probability scores for lesions), and conclusions derived from new data, rather than summarizing prior literature.

title: Three-Dimensional Reconstruction of Basal Cell and Squamous Cell Carcinomas: Noninvasive Evaluation of Cancerous Tissue Cross Sections and Margins
brief_summary: This study developed a noninvasive method to classify basal cell carcinoma (BCC) and squamous cell carcinoma (SCC) using three-dimensional optical coherence tomography (OCT) images and artificial intelligence. The researchers utilized a transfer learning-based convolutional neural network (CNN) with ResNet18 architecture to analyze OCT volume scans collected from patients with Fitzpatrick skin types I and II. The model was trained on grayscale OCT images converted into three-channel inputs, achieving sensitivity and specificity between 92% and 100% for distinguishing cancerous lesions from normal skin. Key results demonstrated that the AI could identify cancerous regions and clear lesion margins by analyzing subchannel pixel intensity losses, particularly in green and blue channels. The findings suggest that this 3D OCT and AI approach can reduce the need for tissue biopsies and improve the precision of surgical margin assessment in dermatology.

lesion_types_studied.details: Studied as a cancerous lesion type; 100 BCC samples were included in the dataset used for CNN model training and testing. Classified alongside SCC and normal skin.
Studied as a cancerous lesion type; 47 SCC samples were included in the dataset used for CNN model training and testing. Classified alongside BCC and normal skin.
Used as the negative control/comparator class for classification; 200 normal skin samples were included in the dataset. The CNN model classified images as normal skin, BCC, or SCC.

all_task_types.target: Discrete diagnostic categories (normal skin, basal cell carcinoma, squamous cell carcinoma)
all_task_types.details: The study uses a transfer learning-based convolutional neural network (CNN) with ResNet18 architecture to classify optical coherence tomography (OCT) images into discrete classes: normal skin, basal cell carcinoma (BCC), or squamous cell carcinoma (SCC). The model outputs probabilities for these classes (e.g., 96.4% probability for BCC), and lesions are defined as cancerous if probabilities exceed 90% or benign if less than 20%. Performance was evaluated using sensitivity, specificity, and ROC-AUC.

datasets_used.name: Rutgers Center for Dermatology and Summit Health OCT Cohort
datasets_used.description: A collection of optical coherence tomography (OCT) volume scan images and grayscale pixel images collected from patients with skin cancers (BCC and SCC) and normal skin. Images were collected in vivo from patients with Fitzpatrick skin types I and II at the Rutgers Center for Dermatology (Somerset, NJ, USA) and Summit Health (Berkeley Heights, NJ, USA). Clinical diagnoses were confirmed by board-certified dermatopathologists via H&E staining of tissue biopsies.

use.training: 80% of the images from this dataset were used for training the transfer learning-based CNN (ResNet18).
use.validation: Used for validation accuracy to save the best-performing model during training (specific count not reported).
use.testing: 20% of the images from this dataset were used for model testing.
use.other: Specimens studied in 3D reconstruction were explicitly not used in the training and testing of the models. The dataset served as the ground truth source for comparing CNN predictions against histopathology.

statistical_methods_used.purpose: To ensure robust performance evaluation of the CNN model during training.
To assess the predictive power of the CNN models for distinguishing cancerous lesions from normal skin.
To evaluate the discriminatory ability of the CNN models.
To provide a detailed breakdown of correct and incorrect classifications by the CNN models.
To define cancerous lesions (probabilities > 90%) and benign lesions (probabilities < 20%) for classification purposes.

hypothesis_testing.interpretation: The paper did not report formal hypothesis testing, p-values, or significance tests. Performance was evaluated using sensitivity, specificity, ROC-AUC, and probability thresholds (e.g., >90% for cancerous, <20% for benign) derived from the CNN model, but no statistical hypothesis testing framework was described.

split.validation: Used for saving best model (count not reported)
split.note: Specimens studied in 3D reconstruction were not used in training/testing

overall_performance_metrics.note: Values reported for predicting BCCs and SCCs from grayscale OCT images

Figure_7_Biopsy_Center.description: Central point of shave biopsy in BCC patient

Rutgers Center for Dermatology and Summi.notes: CNN models correctly identified BCC and SCC in agreement with dermatopathologist diagnosis. Clear lesion edges identified by CNN matched pathology reports. Visual verification of green and blue subchannel intensity losses supported CNN predictions.

architecture_used.pretraining: Transfer learning-based (implies ImageNet pretraining given normalization standards mentioned)
architecture_used.input_size: Converted into three-channel inputs to match ResNet18 requirements (specific dimensions not reported)

architecture_details.modification: Final classification layer modified to output a single node for binary classification
architecture_details.input_processing: Grayscale images split into red, green, and blue subimages; converted to three-channel inputs; normalized following ImageNet standards
architecture_details.data_augmentation: Random horizontal flipping, rotation, and resizing

training_details.model_selection: Best-performing model saved based on validation accuracy

classification_or_output_head.output: Single node for binary classification (normal skin vs. BCC/SCC)

preprocessing_techniques: Grayscale OCT images converted into three-channel inputs (red, green, blue subimages) to match ResNet18 requirements
Data augmentation: random horizontal flipping, rotation, and resizing
Pixel intensity distribution broken into low (green), medium (blue), and high (red) subchannel images using a lookup table
Stacking 2D slices sequentially in correct order for 3D visualization
No interpolation applied to preserve original resolution of OCT data

conclusions: Three-dimensional optical coherence tomography (OCT) cross sections of cancerous lesions can be collected noninvasively.
Artificial intelligence (AI) using convolutional neural networks can classify skin lesions (basal cell carcinoma and squamous cell carcinoma) and detect clear lesion edges noninvasively.
The AI method achieved sensitivity and specificity between 92% and 100% for predicting BCCs and SCCs from grayscale OCT images.
Cancerous regions can be verified visually by observing the loss of green and blue subchannel pixel intensities in color-coded OCT images.
The AI-identified clear lesion edges matched the findings of histopathology reports, confirming the method's accuracy in defining margins.
This noninvasive 3D OCT and AI approach has the potential to reduce the need for tissue biopsies on benign skin lesions.
The method may speed up treatment and promote better patient screening, particularly for older patients who are likely to develop multiple lesions.
important_remarks: The study population is limited to Fitzpatrick skin types I and II, restricting generalizability to darker skin tones.
The dataset is small (347 total samples: 200 normal, 100 BCC, 47 SCC) and lacks external validation from independent centers.
The technique is explicitly limited by the lack of consensus among dermatopathologists on lesion diagnosis, particularly for pigmented lesions like melanomas.
The models are susceptible to overfitting, which the authors note can be mitigated by collecting more clinical data and refining the models.
The study did not include lesions with mixed components, limiting the applicability to complex skin cancers.
There is a potential conflict of interest as one author is an employee and another is a stockholder of OptoVibronex, the company associated with the technology.
No formal statistical hypothesis testing (e.g., p-values) was reported; performance was evaluated using sensitivity, specificity, and ROC-AUC.
The 3D reconstruction specimens were explicitly excluded from the training and testing datasets, preventing data leakage but limiting the direct validation of the 3D model against the CNN classifier.
